questions: Should we migrate existing effects from the `effects` table to the `resources` table, or support both tables during a transition period? If migrating, is there existing user data in the effects table that needs to be preserved?

For the LLM provider: should we use Groq (matching the existing `ai-prompt` pattern) or a different provider? Groq's models (Kimi K2, Llama 3.3) may not be ideal for code generation — would you prefer OpenAI or Anthropic for the effect generation specifically?

For the preview: should it use the currently selected clip as the preview subject, or a generic sample (colored rectangle, stock image)? Using the actual clip would be more useful but adds complexity.

Should the effect creator panel be inline in the ClipPanel (expandable section), a slide-over sheet, or a full dialog/modal? The current CustomEffectEditor is inline but the LLM-powered version will be larger.

Do effects need versioning? If a user edits a saved effect, should it update in-place or create a new version? Clips referencing the old version would silently get the new behavior on update-in-place.
success_criteria: User can select a category (entrance/exit/continuous), type a natural language description, and receive generated Remotion effect code from the LLM edge function
Generated code compiles successfully via the Sucrase pipeline and produces a valid React component
User can preview the generated effect on a sample clip before saving
User can edit an existing effect by describing desired changes in natural language
Saved effects appear in the appropriate category dropdown (entrance/exit/continuous) alongside built-in effects
Effects are stored in the resources table with type 'effect' and proper metadata (name, slug, code, category, description)
Public effects from other users appear in dropdowns; private effects only appear for their creator
Applying a custom effect from the dropdown renders correctly in the Remotion player and final export
Compilation errors from invalid LLM output are shown inline with a retry option
The edge function is rate-limited and authenticated, following the existing ai-prompt pattern
assumptions: We will use Groq as the LLM provider (matching the existing ai-prompt edge function pattern) unless directed otherwise.
The effects table can be fully replaced by the resources table — we'll add a one-time migration for any existing effects data, then deprecate the effects table hooks.

The prompt template will instruct the LLM to use React.createElement (no JSX) to match the existing effectPromptTemplate.ts constraint, since the Sucrase pipeline handles JSX but createElement is more reliable from LLM output.
The effect creator panel will be a dialog/sheet rather than inline, given it includes generation, preview, and code display — too large for the inline ClipPanel.
Effects update in-place (no versioning) — matching current behavior where editing an effect's code immediately affects all clips using it.
The preview will use a simple colored rectangle as the sample subject, not the actual selected clip, to keep the preview lightweight and independent of clip selection.
Public/private toggle will default to private, with an option to make effects public (matching the existing resource visibility pattern).
Rate limiting will reuse the existing RATE_LIMITS infrastructure from the ai-prompt function.
